After looking into this more, I'm a little frustrated with the way the library handles errors. Using a server my company has, which also requires a password, I attempted to connect using an invalid password with this script:

```
import logging
import ssl

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)

import irc.client
import irc.connection

client = irc.client.IRC()
server = client.server()
password = 'foo'
ssl_factory = irc.connection.Factory(wrapper=ssl.wrap_socket)
conn = server.connect(
	'irc.example.net', 7000, 'jaraco_test',
	password=password,
	connect_factory=ssl_factory,
)

def on_error(connection, event):
	print(event.target)
	if 'Password mismatch' in event.target:
		connection.disconnect("You can't fire me; I quit!")
		raise Exception("Failed to authenticate")

client.add_global_handler('error', on_error)
client.process_forever()
```

(I've replaced our actual server and port)

What I get if I run that is this:

```
> python .\bad-pass.py
DEBUG:irc.client:connect(server='irc.example.com', port=7000, nickname='jaraco_test', ...)
DEBUG:irc.client:TO SERVER: PASS foo
DEBUG:irc.client:TO SERVER: NICK jaraco_test
DEBUG:irc.client:TO SERVER: USER jaraco_test 0 * :jaraco_test
DEBUG:irc.client:process_forever(timeout=0.2)
DEBUG:irc.client:FROM SERVER: :irc.example.com NOTICE AUTH :*** Looking up your hostname...
DEBUG:irc.client:command: privnotice, source: irc.example.com, target: AUTH, arguments: ['*** Looking up your hostname...']
DEBUG:irc.client:FROM SERVER: :irc.example.com NOTICE AUTH :*** Found your hostname
DEBUG:irc.client:command: privnotice, source: irc.example.com, target: AUTH, arguments: ['*** Found your hostname']
DEBUG:irc.client:FROM SERVER: ERROR :Closing Link: jaraco_test[c-68-55-72-219.hsd1.dc.comcast.net] (Password mismatch)
DEBUG:irc.client:command: error, source: None, target: Closing Link: jaraco_test[c-68-55-72-219.hsd1.dc.comcast.net] (Password mismatch), arguments: []
Closing Link: jaraco_test[c-68-55-72-219.hsd1.dc.comcast.net] (Password mismatch)
DEBUG:irc.client:TO SERVER: QUIT :You can't fire me; I quit!
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".\bad-pass.py", line 26, in <module>
    client.process_forever()
  File "C:\Users\jaraco\projects\irc\irc\client.py", line 266, in process_forever
    self.process_once(timeout)
  File "C:\Users\jaraco\projects\irc\irc\client.py", line 247, in process_once
    self.process_data(i)
  File "C:\Users\jaraco\projects\irc\irc\client.py", line 212, in process_data
    c.process_data()
  File "C:\Users\jaraco\projects\irc\irc\client.py", line 645, in process_data
    self._handle_event(Event(command, NickMask(prefix), target, arguments))
  File "C:\Users\jaraco\projects\irc\irc\client.py", line 649, in _handle_event
    self.irclibobj._handle_event(self, event)
  File "C:\Users\jaraco\projects\irc\irc\client.py", line 386, in _handle_event
    result = handler.callback(connection, event)
  File ".\bad-pass.py", line 23, in on_error
    raise Exception("Failed to authenticate")
Exception: Failed to authenticate
```

But more interestingly, if I leave out the 'raise Exception' clause, the script will run indefinitely. It will 'process_forever' even though it'll never have any sockets in the main event loop.

So a few things to note. First, the IRC library could probably handle better the circumstance where it doesn't have any connections. Second, the IRC server we use doesn't follow the RFC for handling the invalid password. Third, it was fairly straightforward to write a handler to capture when the password is invalid.

I realize this doesn't "fix" the issue, but it should help anyone struggling with it to better understand how to handle it.

It looks like you installed `virtualenv` and then ran `pipsi install gdbgui`, but `pipsi` was not installed yet.

Try 
```
curl https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mitsuhiko/pipsi/master/get-pipsi.py | python
```
again then open a new terminal and run
```
pipsi install gdbgui
```

If you still get an error about missing `virtualenv`, try running `pip install virtualenv` or `sudo pip install virtualenv`. Then install `pipsi`, then use `pipsi` to install gdbgui.

You can modify the PATH environment variable by modifying the file ~/.bashrc. See this link for more https://stackoverflow.com/questions/13631173/adding-a-path-to-the-bashrc-file, and use google.

Another solution would be to download the binary [here](https://gdbgui.com/downloads/linux/gdbgui_0.13.0.0), then run `chmod +x <file>`, then run it directly.

The formulas are contained in my answer. For a general e,p:

precision=p+1
emax=2**(e-1)
emin=4-emax-precision

Regarding the choice in rouding mode versus the correct answer. I think gmpy2 is returning the correct answer but I could easily be wrong.

>>> ctx=context(precision=4, emax=4, emin=-4, subnormalize=True)
>>> set_context(ctx)
>>> a=mpfr("0.75")
>>> b=mpfr("0.03125")
>>> a.digits(2)
('1100', 0, 4)
>>> b.digits(2)
('1000', -4, 4)

Let's temporarily change the working precision:

>>> get_context().precision=16
>>> c=a+b
>>> c.digits(2)
('1100100000000000', 0, 16)

Then use round2 to round the result to 4 bits of precision:

>>> round2(c,4)
mpfr('0.75',4)
>>> round2(c,4).digits(2)
('1100', 0, 4)

Since RoundToNearest (equivalent to IEEE roundTiesToEven) requires the last bit of result is 0 when the exact value is halfway between two representable values, we get the value.

If the value for a is changed to end with a 1 bit, then rounding changes and the result is rounded up.

>>> get_context().precision=4
>>> d=a+mpq(1)/16
>>> d.digits(2)
('1101', 0, 4)
>>> (d+b).digits(2)
('1110', 0, 4)

For curio there are mentions of "one-man project"
flavours around the web and even here. I strongly
disagree with such undervalueing words.

Simplicy is the most valuable thing for Curio, which
very understandably can be done with a clever
man. So please stop "its a small project"
attitude starting here.

I were a heavy user of asyncio and in search for the
so called "right style", I experimented various flow
patterns and carried some customizations
to asyncio which became unnecessarily complicated
over time.

At that point I settled downto this:
"Async code should be identical to blocking code."
(Except for async defs, awaits, etc and some
management code.)

This style is supported by Curio out of the box which
is the main reason for my absolute migration..

Thanks for the clever work and please never feel
"sorry" for the small code base.

Infact, ideally and asymtotically there will be
"No Async Library", as languages will become
transparently async.

Of course this is an utopia.

It seems like fish and bash both have the `history` command. The issue is that in fish the history is in reverse-chronological order, but in bash it's in chronological order. In bash it's prefixed with a number, in fish it's not. It requires detecting what shell you are using and testing with different shells. I don't think it's impossible. It's just harder.

Hi Paul! 

I work with Semio at [Gapminder](https://www.gapminder.org/), where we promote a fact-based world view using [data-viz](https://www.gapminder.org/tools/) to make the facts understandable : ). We use daff in git to merge all kinds of datasources to create one big dataset describing the world in time series. We love daff and we use it with joy! So, to reiterate on the problem described by @semio :

`daff merge` outputs csv's with CRLF, according to the RFC. This confuses git in linux, because it expects LF, and now there's an extra CR character before the LF

code: https://github.com/paulfitz/daff/blob/053d14c79f4d52c18ce3651dfd08d842f4681086/coopy/Csv.hx#L54
CRLF in csv RFC: https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4180#section-2

options as we see them now:
- ask you, Paul, to allow other line-endings in daff output (doesn't follow spec, though might be better supported nonetheless)
- force CRLF for csv files in gitattributes (might confuse/be overwritten by other linux apps)
- keep manually removing CR's from daff merge results (big bother, not user friendly)
- disable daff merge (but this will make merges a lot less compact and will lead to many more merge conflicts)

So, on the line-ending support of `daff`, I guess it'd be best if daff would copy git behaviour: use the line endings that were originally in the files before `daff merge`. That way, whatever is set in gitattributes for line ending handling will be observed (e.g. linux with crlf forced).

What do you think of this specific way of handling? I'd love to help, but my knowledge of haxe is kind of non-existent.

One of the main reasons for green to exist is to be a fast test runner.  Running in multiple processes is part of that.  If that means some people are turned off and don't want to use it because their existing tests can't be safely run concurrently, I'm okay with that.

If you personally want to use green and have it default to 1 process, you can create a config file (either `$HOME/.green` or export an environment variable `GREEN_CONFIG` which points to some other location) and put this in it:

```
processes = 1
```

...or of course you could make your own fork (or just alter your local module on-disk) and change the default on line 46 of `green/config.py` to `1` ;-)

In order to call remote methods, an interface description is required.
Otherwise, the client code wouldn't know how to encode the arguments. The
interface can either be introspected on-the-fly or provided explicitly as
you're doing in your working code. Generally speaking, it's better to
provide an explicit interface as introspection isn't required by the DBus
specification and it only works if the object in question is online at the
time of the query. It's good for development and testing but I wouldn't
recommend relying upon it in production.

As to the API documentation, there isn't a well-documented API separate
from the tutorial. The docstrings are all there in the code but I never
figured out how to generate a separate doc with Lore. It might "just work"
but I haven't tried it. I recommend just browsing the modules on Github
directly. It's not quite as convenient as a standalone document but the
docstrings stick out pretty well and it's not a particularly large codebase.

Unfortunately I cannot accept it because we cannot assume that a file ending in `mpeg` will necessarily contain ID3 meta data. An `mpeg` file will most of the time be a mpeg1, mpeg2 or mpeg4 video stream and even if there is mp3 audio in the file it might be muxed in many different ways.

Anyway: If you want to force TinyTag to open a file ending in `mpeg` using the ID3 parser, just use the parser class directly instead of calling `TinyTag.get(filename)` e.g.: `ID3.get(filename)`

I was actually able to implement this by setting up a tunnel for each bastion.
The target of the first tunnel is the second bastion, and so on, until the last tunnel which target is the real one.
The bastion for each tunnel is then the local_address of the previous one
After very qick tests, it seems to work properly. I was just wondering if that could be part of the standard sshtunnel.

Here is an extract of my start() code:
```python
self.tunnels = []
for idx, info in enumerate(self.tunnel_info):
    # if we're not the first element, set the bastion to the local port of the previous tunnel
    if idx > 0:
        info['ssh_address_or_host'] = ('localhost', self.tunnels[-1].local_bind_port)
    # if we are the last element, the target is the real target
    if idx == len(self.tunnel_info) - 1:
        target = (self.target_ip, self.target_port)
    # else, the target is the next bastion
    else:
        if isinstance(self.tunnel_info[idx+1]['ssh_address_or_host'], tuple):
            target = self.tunnel_info[idx+1]['ssh_address_or_host']
        else:
            target = (self.tunnel_info[idx+1]['ssh_address_or_host'], 22)
    logger.debug('Setting up SSH tunnel: target = %s, settings = %s', target, info)
    self.tunnels.append(SSHTunnelForwarder(remote_bind_address=target, **info))
    self.tunnels[idx].start()
    logger.debug('  SSH tunnel bound to local port %d', self.tunnels[idx].local_bind_port)
```

And during the stop(), tunnels are being closed in the reverse order:
````python
for tunnel in reversed(self.tunnels):
    logger.debug('Stopping SSH tunnel %s', tunnel.local_bind_address)
    tunnel.stop()
````

@irfan-zoefit It won't and **should not** work with unicode. It clearly says it expects string as an input. That's what verbose errors are for. Also, there are 2 approaches to taking inputs for any library/method. 
1. Take any type of input and convert it to the expected datatype (string in this case)
2. Accept just a single type of input and have verbose errors in case the input is not of the expected type.

Right now, w2n goes with 2. If it goes with 1 in future, sure I will add auto-convert to string. But right now that's kind of ambiguous and less specific. 

Thanks anyway.

Since tabula-java JSON output has several extra data as `extraction_method`, `height`, `width`, `left`, `top`, you can get the position of a table via JSON format, while DataFormat format doesn't include the position.

Here is the example code: 

```py
# data.pdf is included in the repository.
In [5]: tables = read_pdf("./examples/data.pdf", output_format="json", page=2)

In [9]: top = tables[0]['top']

In [10]: left = tables[0]['left']

In [11]: bottom = tables[0]['height'] + top

In [12]: right = tables[0]['width'] + left

In [13]: top, bottom, left, right
Out[13]: (0.0, 528.8800048828125, 0.0, 564.8800048828125)
```

I’m fairly certain you should not be doing that. May I ask what you’re trying to achieve? There may be an existing [XEP](https://xmpp.org/extensions/) which does what you want.

Usually, when extending stanzas, you’d add child elements. You can do that by patching the Message class:

```python
import aioxmpp.stanza
import aioxmpp.xso

aioxmpp.stanza.Message.performative = aioxmpp.xso.ChildText(
    ("your-namespace", "performative"),
    default="query",
)
```

Alternatively, if you **have** to use an attribute (e.g. if there’s a system you absolutely cannot change and with which you have to work), you can do so similarly:

```python
import aioxmpp.stanza
import aioxmpp.xso

aioxmpp.stanza.Message.performative = aioxmpp.xso.Attr(
    "performative",
    default="query",
)
```

Note that inheriting from ``aioxmpp.stanza.Message`` won’t work, because the XMLStream (or rather, the StanzaStream) only knows about ``aioxmpp.stanza.Message`` and will only try to deserialise that (or one of the other stanza classes).

Telepot does not have such a function, but you can always make your own. Let me walk you through my thinking process. 

Normally, you would do it like this:

```python
bot = Delegator.Bot(TOKEN, [
    pave_event_space()(
        per_callback_query_origin(), create_open, SubclassOfCallbackQueryOriginHandler, timeout=10),
])
```

`per_callback_query_origin()` ensures only one object of `SubclassOfCallbackQueryOriginHandler` is spawned for each *callback query origin*, which is the message that gives rise to such callback query. The origin can be an inline message or a regular chat message. Since you are asking to differentiate between chat id, I assume your callback query are originated from regular chat messages.

`per_callback_query_origin()` fails to satisfy you because it does not differentiate between chat id. To do that, you can add some logic surrounding `per_callback_query_origin()`, in effect inventing your own **[seeder](http://telepot.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#delegatorbot)** function:

```python
from telepot.delegate import per_callback_query_origin, _wrap_none

def per_callback_query_origin_for_chat_id_in(s):
    origin_extractor = per_callback_query_origin()

    def f(msg):
        if msg['message']['chat']['id'] in s:
            # act like per_callback_query_origin() only if chat id satisfy your requirement
            return origin_extractor(msg)
        else:
            return None

    # Not every message has the field ['message']['chat']['id'].
    # Use _wrap_none() to catch KeyError and return None instead.
    return _wrap_none(f)
```

Then, use it like this:

```python
some_chat_ids = set()

bot = Delegator.Bot(TOKEN, [
    pave_event_space()(
        per_callback_query_origin_for_chat_id_in(some_chat_ids), create_open, SubclassOfCallbackQueryOriginHandler, timeout=10),
])
```

`per_callback_query_origin_for_chat_id_except(set)` can be implemented likewise.

I have not tested the above code, but that's how I think about it. Hopefully it's not far off from what you want. Good luck.

Look, it is the `ipaddr` target. In the transaction `ipaddr` is empty, `[]`. It means that IPDB expects the interface to have no addresses, but the interface still has some addresses.

It may happen e.g. if ip addresses are added in the very moment the `commit()` operates on them, as the `ipaddr` field in the interface (not transaction) object is continuously updated in the runtime from the system, and normally it should not cause an error (but there is still some possibility, since we can not lock the Linux kernel, thus races are still possible with other programs).

So again it seems to be a race, but a different one. May it be that the NetworkManager runs on the same system?

I'll try to provide tomorrow a branch with the full debug in the IPDB, but not sure how bad it will affect the performance, and there is a possibility — if it is a race condition — that the debug logging by itself will shadow the error by slowing down the core.

Whatever you got that code from is horribly outdated.  https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/tree/master/examples

Those explicit keyword argument names are not going to change since the way they are intended to be used and supported is through the interfaces provided such as [`commands.check`](https://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/rewrite/ext/commands/api.html#discord.ext.commands.check) or [`commands.cooldown`](https://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/rewrite/ext/commands/api.html#discord.ext.commands.cooldown) interfaces.

Providing a subclass should only be through [`commands.Command`](https://rapptz.github.io/discord.py/docs/ext/commands/api.html#discord.ext.commands.Command) while calling `super().__init__` like usual.

So the intended way to do some subclassing should just be something similar to this:

```py
class MyCommand(commands.Command):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.custom_thing = kwargs.pop('custom_thing', None)

@commands.command(cls=MyCommand)
@commands.check(lambda ctx: True)
async def foo(ctx, *, arg: str):
    pass
```

(this assumes v1.0 of the lib).

Cogs are relatively simple (they're just classes). I suggest joining the [official discord.py server](https://discord.gg/r3sSKJJ) for more help on the topic until more proper documentation is released.

You can set all edges in a given direction at a time:

```python
import numpy as np
import maxflow

def create_structure(ioff, joff):
    res = np.zeros((3, 3))
    res[ioff+1, joff+1] = 1
    return res

img = np.random.random_sample(size=(10, 10))
i, j = [_[1:-1, 1:-1].flatten() for _ in np.indices(img.shape)]

graph = maxflow.Graph[float]()
nodeids = graph.add_grid_nodes(img.shape)

for ioff, joff in [(-1, 0), (0, -1), (1, 0), (0, 1)]:
    structure = create_structure(ioff, joff)
    i1 = i + ioff
    j1 = j + joff
    weights = compute_weight(img, i, j, i1, j1)
    # I'm assuming weights has the same shape as nodeids. Crop/reshape otherwise.
    graph.add_grid_edges(nodeids, weights, structure)
```

Of course, a better solution for your case would be having a function that accepts arrays of src nodes, dst nodes and corresponding weights. That would be memory inefficient though, since you have to create several arrays (srsc, dsts and weights) several times larger than the original image. For very large images that might be unfeasible. Or alternatively, just `dup` around in the same process. This does not break e.g. `gtk` clipboard and is probably safe enough:

```python
import os
import pyperclip

def copy_to_clipboard(text):
    stdoutfd_dup = os.dup(1)
    fdnull = os.open(os.devnull, os.O_WRONLY)
    os.dup2(fdnull, 1)
    try:
        pyperclip.copy(text)
    finally:
        # Restore stdout FD and close temporary ones
        os.dup2(stdoutfd_dup, 1)
        os.close(stdoutfd_dup)
        os.close(fdnull)
```

I have thought a little bit about this and want to propose this new `BusABC` implementation:

```Python

from abc import ABCMeta, abstractmethod

class BusABC():
    """A sketch to show the suggested design.

    The comments/docs/implementation are of course shortened
    to make it more readable. In the release this would be
    documented way better.

    New interfaces would need to implement:

        * __init__()
        * _recv_internal()
        * send()
        * set channel_info atribute

    That is pretty much the same as perviously, only that
    recv() is now called _recv_internal() and must return
    `(msg, False)` instead of only `msg` if it didn't support
    filters before and `(msg, True)` if it did.

    It doesn't change *ANY* method signatures used by the actual
    users of the library, and only requires minimal changes in
    the infterfaces.
    """

    @abstractmethod
    def __init__(self, channel=None, can_filters=None, **config):
        """Same as always, except it now has to call self._apply_filters()
        insteat of setting the field itself."""
        self._apply_filters(can_filters)

    def recv(self, timeout=None):
        """Not abstract any more."""

        msg, already_filtered = self._recv_internal(timeout=timeout)

        if msg and (already_filtered or self._matches_filter(msg)):
            return msg

        else:
            # discard message & try next one if there still is time

            elapsed_time = 0 # actually measure this in the real implementation

            if elapsed_time >= timeout:
                return None
            else:
                # is a call stack overflow a real scenario here?
                return self.rcev(timeout=timeout - elapsed_time)

    @abstractmethod
    def _recv_internal(self, timeout=None):
        """The old implementation of recv() moves to here.
        This method returns a tuple (msg, already_filtered)
        containing the message that was rceived (or None) and
        whether it was already filtered by the bus (for example
        in a kernel driver or even in real hardware).
        """
        pass

    def _matches_filter(self, msg):
        """Checks whether the given message matches one of the current filters."""
        for filter in self.filters:
            return True # do the actual checking
        # else
        return False

    def set_filters(self, filters):
        """Not pseudo-abstract any more.
        Now ALL interfaces support filters."""
        self.filters = filters
        self._apply_filters(filters)

    # These methods/fields remain exactly as they are
    def __str__(self): pass
    def send(self, msg): pass # still @abstractmethod
    def send_periodic(self, msg): pass
    def __iter__(self): pass
    def flush_tx_buffer(self): pass
    def shutdown(self): pass
    channel_info = 'unknown'
    __metaclass__ = ABCMeta

```

About the rather unusual return value of `_recv_internal() -> (msg, already_filtered)`. There were two approaches that came to my mind: 

1.) The `BusABC` class could have a private attribute (maybe `_supports_low_level_filtering`) that is `True` if the bus implementation supports kernel level or hardware level filters and else is set to `False`. And based on that `recv()` could figure out whether it has to filter in software or whether filtering has already been done.

2.) The approach I have shown above is a bit more flexible since it might for example allow normal IDs to be filtered in hardware but not extended IDs.

I can probably counter those arguments (and will later on), but what it comes done to is a philosophical question on wheather or not a seperate constants file is needed for a project this size. As you know, I come from Home Assistant and that's the way we do things there and I personally see certain benefits in it which is why I suggested the changes in the first place.

Regarding the namespace: With using `from .const import (...)` only those constants specified really get imported, so you don't have any issues there.

Point 2: That might be true if constants are used in one module only. However this is not always the case although the grade varies.

Point 3: The idea behind constants is that the name should tell you everything you need to know about them. E.g. you shouldn't care if it's `minValue`, `MinValue` or `min_value`. Furthermore a separate module can easily opend in split screen mode to view them beside the original module.

Point 4: Spelling in this case is meant for writing `'characteristics'` multiple times for the HAP representation. If you make a mistake yu would only notice it during runtime. Sometimes faster sometimes slower. With constants a speeling mistake would lead to a ValueError or ImportError during complation. So it's a bit more likely to be found fast.

Point 5: Your're right here. However to some degree this is managed be giving fitting names like `HAP_FORMAT_****`, etc.

This file from APS 6-ID-D has a pathological error.

The #L line in SPEC uses a double-space (or more) separator between 
labels so as to allow multiword labels to use a single space separator. 
  Such as this example which describes three column labels:

#L two theta  I00  counts

It seems there is a local SPEC macro override that rewrites how the #L 
line is written to the file.

It is possible to change how spec2nexus interprets the #L line but that 
would cause greater disruption to the many files which relay on a 
double-space to separate column labels.

To read this file with spec2nexus, a custom control line handler plugin 
should be written.  The custom plugin should copy the existing class

   class SPEC_Labels(ControlLineHandler):

from <spec2nexus>/plugins/spec_common_spec2nexus.py and modify this line:

         # Some folks use more than two spaces!  Use regular 
expression(re) module
         scan.L = re.split("  +", strip_first_word(text))

to fit the situation.

Another possibility is for spec2nexus to develop a list of #L handlers 
to try, in succession, for this rare but obvious pathology.

Pete

def translate_text(chatbot, statement, target):
    """Translates text into the target language.

    Target must be an ISO 639-1 language code.
    See https://g.co/cloud/translate/v2/translate-reference#supported_languages
    """
    from google.cloud import translate
    
    translate_client = translate.Client()

    # Text can also be a sequence of strings, in which case this method
    # will return a sequence of results for each text.
    result = translate_client.translate(
        statement.text,
        target_language=target)
    
    # Input language: result['input']
    # Translated language: result['translatedText']
    # Detected source language: result['detectedSourceLanguage']
    statement.text = str(result['translatedText'])
    return statement
```

I've kinda been wondering why pyshp doesn't support the [shapefile .CPG file format](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shapefile#Overview) for specifying the DBF file's codepage.

GDAL reads the .cpg file in the [dbfopen.c driver](https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/trunk/gdal/ogr/ogrsf_frmts/shape/dbfopen.c) and stores its contents verbatim in the `psDBF->pszCodePage`.  Then, it translated it using [ConvertCodePage()](https://github.com/OSGeo/gdal/blob/8f4a9197084c543cde9ce534cc63f83d03fe6a2c/gdal/ogr/ogrsf_frmts/shape/ogrshapelayer.cpp#L279).  

Perhaps one issue is the [Python standard encoding](https://docs.python.org/3/library/codecs.html#standard-encodings) aren't always the same names.   

I also think that an `encoding` parameter for Reader/Writer, as suggest, would also be a good approach.

After fixing on Jun 4, this issue seems to be unfixed in the replacement of `_set_attribute()` by `_set_new_attribute()`. The latter's result (`True` if attribute already exists) is silently ignored for `__init__`, `__repr__`, and `__eq__` (but not for other comparators like `__lt__` and attribute accessors like `__setattr__`, which still raise "`cannot overwrite`" exceptions).
Don't know whether this was on purpose, but if so it may need documenting to prevent surprised users.

Fwiw, if it helps anyone, I put this workaround patch into the Debian package of flask-htmlmin to get pgadmin4 working:
```
--- a/flask_htmlmin.py
+++ b/flask_htmlmin.py
@@ -1,4 +1,6 @@
+import logging
 from htmlmin import Minifier
+from htmlmin.parser import OpenTagNotFoundError
 
 __author__ = 'Hamid FzM'
 
@@ -31,9 +33,13 @@ class HTMLMIN(object):
         """
         if response.content_type == u'text/html; charset=utf-8':
             response.direct_passthrough = False
-            response.set_data(
-                self.html_minify.minify(response.get_data(as_text=True))
-            )
+            data = response.get_data(as_text=True)
+            try:
+                minified = self.html_minify.minify(data)
+            except OpenTagNotFoundError:
+                minified = data
+                logging.error('Could not minify data: OpenTagNotFoundError')
+            response.set_data(minified)
 
             return response
         return response
```

Just to mention another bonus of having a centralized repo for panflute filters: the naming scheme for filters can be shorter. Currently, people called the filters like `pandoc-includes`, `pandoc-csv2tables`, `pandoc-placetables`, `pandoc-amsthm`, etc. because they are submitted to `cabal`/`pip`, etc. and the prepended `pandoc` is for identification among the seas of packages. If the panflute filters fall in one repo, the prepended string won't be necessary, which allow a cleaner, shortner name.

最新发布的 0.26.0 版本初步解决了这个问题：

```python
>>> from pypinyin import pinyin, Style
>>> pinyin('南岗子街', style=Style.TONE3)
[['nan2'], ['gang3'], ['zi'], ['jie1']]
>>> pinyin('大地', style=Style.TONE3)
[['da4'], ['di4']]
>>> pinyin('东大地', style=Style.TONE3)
[['dong1'], ['da4'], ['di4']]
>>> pinyin('大都市街', style=Style.TONE3)
[['da4'], ['du1'], ['shi4'], ['jie1']]
>>> pinyin('长青路', style=Style.TONE3)
[['chang2'], ['qing1'], ['lu4']]
>>> pinyin('佛陀', style=Style.TONE3)
[['fo2'], ['tuo2']]
>>> pinyin('佛寺', style=Style.TONE3)
[['fo2'], ['si4']]
>>> pinyin('大佛寺', style=Style.TONE3)
[['da4'], ['fu2'], ['si4']]
>>> pinyin('长安街', style=Style.TONE3)
[['chang2'], ['an1'], ['jie1']]
>>>
```

其中

```python
>>> pinyin('南岗子街', style=Style.TONE3)
[['nan2'], ['gang3'], ['zi'], ['jie1']]
```

是因为没有词库中没有 `南岗子街`/`南岗子` /`岗子` 不知道它的正确读音是啥，用的是`子`的默认读音 `zi`。

```python
>>> pinyin('大佛寺', style=Style.TONE3)
[['da4'], ['fu2'], ['si4']]
```

是因为词库里没有 `大佛寺`/`大佛` 用的是`佛` 的默认读音 `fú`。

自定义词库可以参考文档：http://pypinyin.mozillazg.com/zh_CN/master/usage.html#id6

Ok, here's the problem. The DRF DefaultRouter class handles adding the .format variants in its `get_urls()` method (https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/master/rest_framework/routers.py#L320).

Because `NestedSimpleRouter`s are distinct trees of URLs that are included separately, this final format_suffix_patterns step is not executed.

I can see two possible solutions -- 

1) add
```
if self.include_format_suffixes:
            urls = format_suffix_patterns(urls)
```
to the `NestedSimpleRouter`'s `get_url()` method. Not very generic, but looks like it will work. Sketch of this is here: https://github.com/alanjds/drf-nested-routers/pull/71

2) Come up with a cunning way to arrange for `default_router.urls` to include the URLs from nested routers that have been attached under it; this would mean that you'd no longer do 
```
urlpatterns = patterns('',
    url(r'^', include(router.urls)),
    url(r'^', include(domains_router.urls)),
)
```
and instead the first include() would add all the URLs. Then the `DefaultRouter` would be responsible for generating the format suffixes on the nested routers' URLs.

2) sounds a bit more tricky, but potentially more robust to future changes in upstream code; if you have any thoughts on that approach I'd be interested to hear. That said, looks like my quick solution 1) above has done the trick, so I may just stick with that (or subclassing NestedSimpleRouter if that patch isn't accepted upstream)

Well, I actually have to disagree with you... I'll explain.

When I coded pytest-logging I was looking for an easy way to get *all* logging, before, during and after running the test suite, for all libraries involved. This is something that is most likely to be used while developing tests or while debugging why a test started to fail, not all the time.
However, on a CI context, I'm most likely interested on the logging that happened while the test was being prep'ed till the end of it, that's why `CatchLogHandler` uses a TextIO stream(to get back the stream contents and include them in the report).
The console stream is "attached" to `sys.stderr`, you can't get back it's contents.

So either e attach 2 stream to CatchLogHandler(in case verbosity is higher than  1) and write to both streams, or, we have separate handlers(which actually serve 2 different purposes....)
Since this plugin merged to PyTest itself, the report moved to PyTest tracker [here](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/3062).

Hello NitroCode,
here is an example, how you could change custom fields with scope _specification_ for all test cases of one specific project

```
from testlink import TestlinkAPIClient, TestLinkHelper
from testlink.testlinkerrors import TLResponseError
import sys, os.path

# precondition 
# SERVER_URL and KEY are defined in environment
# TESTLINK_API_PYTHON_SERVER_URL=http://YOURSERVER/testlink/lib/api/xmlrpc/v1/xmlrpc.php
# TESTLINK_API_PYTHON_DEVKEY=7ec252ab966ce88fd92c25d08635672b
tl_helper = TestLinkHelper()
myTestLink = TestlinkAPIClient(tl_helper._server_url, tl_helper._devkey, 
                               verbose=False)

def iterTCasesfromTProject(api, TPid):
    """ returns as iterator all test cases for project TPid """
    deep=1
    details='only_id'
    for TSinfo in api.getFirstLevelTestSuitesForTestProject(TPid):
        print(" Iter suite", TSinfo['name'], "with ID", TSinfo['id'])
        for TCid in api.getTestCasesForTestSuite(TSinfo['id'], deep, details):
            TCdata = api.getTestCase(TCid)[0] #really only one TC?
            print("  TC %(full_tc_external_id)s Version %(version)s %(creation_ts)s - %(name)s " % TCdata)
            yield TCdata

# loop over all test cases for project - 'NEW_PROJECT_API-34'
# changes the custom field 'cf_tc_sd_string' of these cases to 'newValue'
#  !! works only for custom field with scope 'Specification' !!
TProjectName = 'NEW_PROJECT_API-34'
CFname='cf_tc_sd_string'
CFvalue='newValue8'

TPinfo = myTestLink.getTestProjectByName(TProjectName)
TPid = TPinfo['id']
print("For all test cases of project", TPinfo['name'], "with ID", TPid)
print("there custom field", CFname, "will be updated to", CFvalue)

for TCdata in iterTCasesfromTProject(myTestLink, TPid):
    TCexID = TCdata['full_tc_external_id']
    TCversion = int(TCdata['version'])
    oldCFvalue = myTestLink.getTestCaseCustomFieldDesignValue(
                            TCexID, TCversion, TPid, CFname, 'value')
    myTestLink.updateTestCaseCustomFieldDesignValue(
                            TCexID, TCversion, TPid, {CFname : CFvalue})
    newCFvalue= myTestLink.getTestCaseCustomFieldDesignValue(
                            TCexID, TCversion, TPid, CFname, 'value')
    print("   CF value changed", oldCFvalue, "->", newCFvalue)
```
For other examples, please see [TestLinkExample.py](https://github.com/lczub/TestLink-API-Python-client/blob/master/example/TestLinkExample.py) and [TestLinkExample_CF_KW.py](https://github.com/lczub/TestLink-API-Python-client/blob/master/example/TestLinkExample_CF_KW.py)

Yep so the initial the plan is to first add support for the various event sources as new decorators.  These handle the linking of the resource to the lambda function (and associated policies) along with any code abstractions to make working with the event easier.  #516 comes into play where it can also create the resource for you.  So as an example, you can have:

```python
@app.on_sns_publish(topic_arn='arn:foo:...')
def handler(event):
   pass
```

This assume the SNS topic already exists, but chalice will handle creating the lambda function, subscribing the lambda function to the topic if needed, and updating any policies.  I'm working on a branch that adds support for the various event sources we've had requests for including sns, s3, kinesis, and sqs (once it officially launches).  The resources proposal in #516 means it would also create the SNS topic (or s3 bucket, kinesis stream, sqs queue, etc) for you.

This PR has a number of areas where I expect to get feedback from the group.  However, it covers an area currently under-served by soco, Sonos Playlist management.  

The underlying service is complex and not well documented and i did my best to tame it but still retain the ability to use it optimally.  I've written simplified helper functions for the 3 most common operations, remove a track, move a track and clear the Sonos playlist.  

Areas of note:
 * Indexing - I've used 0-based indexing.  The underlying services refer to the first item in a Sonos Playlist as 0, Python lists use 0 based indexing.  This is in line with the other 'queue' management methods.

* update_id - I am still unsure just how important it is, but the existing add_item_to_sonos_playlist methods deemed it important.  I allow the calling code to set it so a lookup can be avoided, the input parameter is optional and defaults to 0.  I also return it as part of every Sonos playlist mgmt method so the caller can pass it on to the next call if a multi operation is being done.

* object_id - allows for a simple string with the item_id but also accepts an object that has an item_id attribute. I wanted to make it simple for the caller to use what they had available.

* tracks/new_pos 
  * reorder_sonos_playlists - takes a list of ints, a list of int-like or a string containing a comma separated list of numbers.  The tracks/new_pos parameters are expected to be of the same logical type.
  * helper functions accept simple int or int-likes.  (i.e. 1 vs '1')

# convert to jsonl format
file_number = 0
for i, doc in enumerate(documents, 1):
    with open("documents-{:0>4}.jsonl".format(file_number), "a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(doc) + "\n")
    if i % 10 == 0:
        file_number += 1

# replacement for get_minibatch that
# 1. takes a bag
# 2. returns just the X_text (haven't figured out y yet)

def get_minibatch2(documents, pos_class=positive_class):
    """Extract a minibatch of examples, return a tuple X_text, y.

    Note: size is before excluding invalid docs with no topics assigned.

    """
    data = [(u'{title}\n\n{body}'.format(**doc), pos_class in doc['topics'])
            for doc in documents if doc['topics']]
    if not len(data):
        return np.asarray([], dtype=int), np.asarray([], dtype=int)
    X_text, y = zip(*data)
    return X_text  # TODO: y
```

@uranusjr That's what I meant by gathering effort. Python desperately need a good package manager like cargo. The Python ecosystem pales in comparison with the other languages due to the lack of a standard way to do things. And `pipenv` will not help with that, I think.

What bothers me is that `pipenv` advertise itself as `the officially recommended Python packaging tool` while it's not a packaging tool, far from it, which is misleading for users. It's merely a dependency manager coupled with a virtualenv manager.

Also, you say that it was inspired by cargo, npm, yarn which are packaging tools along with dependency managers while piping is not.

And here is the flaw of `pipenv`, it just muddies the water since people will still make the same mistakes as before with `requirements.txt` vs `setup.py`. Projects will still be badly packaged with badly define dependencies in their `setup.py` because of that. That's what projects like cargo did right: they handle all the aspects of developing projects/applications to ensure a consistency while a project like `pipenv` does not.

And when you say:

> which Pipenv and poetry both do (and neither perfectly)

What do you mean? From what I have seen, their dependency manager is much more resilient than the one provided by `pipenv`. The only downside is that they use the PyPI JSON API which sometimes does not have dependency information due to badly published packages.

Anyway, I think, like you said, that both projects can learn from each other.

And, one more thing, what's the future of pipenv if, ultimately, pip handles the `Pipfile`? Will it just be a virtualenv manager?

Command

Arguments
    --condition         [Required]: The condition expression upon which to trigger the rule.
        Usage:   --condition "METRIC {>,>=,<,<=} THRESHOLD {avg,min,max,total,last} PERIOD"
        Example: --condition "Percentage CPU > 60 avg 1h30m".
    --name -n           [Required]: Name of the alert rule.

                                    using `az configure --defaults group=<name>`.
    --description                 : Free-text description of the rule. Defaults to the condition
                                    expression.
    --disabled                    : Create the rule in a disabled state.  Allowed values: false,
                                    true.
    --location -l                 : Location. You can configure the default location using `az
                                    configure --defaults location=<location>`.
    --tags                        : Space separated tags in 'key[=value]' format. Use "" to clear
                                    existing tags.

Action Arguments
    --action -a                   : Add an action to fire when the alert is triggered.
        Usage:   --add-action TYPE KEY [ARG ...]
        Email:   --add-action email bob@contoso.com ann@contoso.com
        Webhook: --add-action webhook https://www.contoso.com/alert apiKey=value
        Webhook: --add-action webhook https://www.contoso.com/alert?apiKey=value
        To specify multiple actions, add multiple --add-action ARGS entries.
    --email-service-owners        : Email the service owners if an alert is triggered.  Allowed
                                    values: false, true.

Target Resource Arguments
    --target            [Required]: Name or ID of the target resource.
    --target-namespace            : Target resource provider namespace.
    --target-parent               : Target resource parent path, if applicable.
    --target-type                 : Target resource type. Can also accept namespace/type format (Ex:
                                    'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines)').

Examples
    Create a High CPU-based alert on a VM with no actions.
        az vm alert rule create -n rule1 -g <RG> --target <VM ID> --condition "Percentage CPU > 90
        avg 5m"


    Create a High CPU-based alert on a VM with email and webhook actions.
        az vm alert rule create -n rule1 -g <RG> --target <VM ID> \
            --condition "Percentage CPU > 90 avg 5m" \
            --action email bob@contoso.com ann@contoso.com --email-service-owners \
            --action webhook https://www.contoso.com/alerts?type=HighCPU \
            --action webhook https://alerts.contoso.com apiKey=<KEY> type=HighCPU

Hi,

thanks for the quick response. Is there a recommended way to test this kind of error? As far as I can see, the *error* tests in `test/crut_test.py` connect to an actual documentdb instance and rely on an invalid request, such missing permissions. Testing that a request-rate exceeded error is handled correctly using an actual documentdb instance would depend a lot on timing (and the connection speed of the machine the test is running on).

So, basically, I could see two approaches:

- Spam a documentdb collection with ~50k requests as fast as possible, and hope that at least one of them throws a rate limit exceeded/retry-after. So basically, that something like ```for i in range(0, 50000): document_client.query("SELECT * FROM coll_0")``` does not throw an exception (?)
- Fake/Mock the connection to the documentdb, for example by creating something like a `MockHTTPsConnection`, and overwriting the `HTTPsConnection` class in the `pydocumentdb.https_connection` module for the unit test.

What would you recommend? Are there maybe similar tests in the dotnet/java documentdb clients? (I had a look, but couldn't find any....)

Sorry, that clause definitely deserved a comment. I originally had this:

...
and name[0] != '_'
...
```
but there is another test case which asserts that defining "__all__" inside a local scope without using it is an error. Personally, i wouldn't consider any unused dunder dunder variable to be an error, but I didn't want to break that test case either, so I changed the above to
```
...
and (name[0:1] != '_' or name[0:2] == '__')
...
```
to get the __all__ test case to pass again.

So if I'm understanding correctly, you are trying to adapt `Checker()` to support checking subsets of code rather than full modules. `ARGUMENTS` is necessary for this new use case.

And this would be a minimal version of the initial example:

`foo.py`:
```python
import ast

import pyflakes.checker


tree = ast.parse("""\
def bar():
    pass
""")
pyflakes.checker.Checker(tree.body[0])
```

    handler = self.getNodeHandler(node.__class__)

Is this the only such hurdle to checking a subset of the code? I would imagine you would run into other problems given that `Checker()` is meant to consume the AST of a module. For example, `self.scopeStack` is initialized to `ModuleScope()`.

> AFAICT there never should have been a 401 on trying to access that URL (Repository issue?) since it is publicly accessible.

That's not true actually.  Conda doesn't inherently know whether a channel is public or not, so if there's a token available, it sends the token.  File an issue with the repo folks if you want to refine some of the server's behavior here :)

And actually, what I stated in the above paragraph is wrong.  A channel itself is always public.  Permissions and visibility are at the individual package level.  Something that I'm pretty adamant about being changed in the v5 platform.  It's a horrible user experience when packages appear and disappear based on different token permissions.  A channel as a whole should either be accessible or not.  And if not, I can then cleanly and clearly tell the user that they just don't have permission to access that channel.

The down and dirty: 
1) Check to see if activate works in cmd.exe.
2) If doesn't work or not acceptable--as @TurboTim shows:
Powershell needs the path to each env (anaconda3\envs\someenv\py33.exe. Laborious! :p
If you don't mind polluting your powershell a little, you can create a profile script which is run every time you open powershell. The below will add the functions `Invoke-CmdScript, CondaActivate, CondaDeactivate` to your powershell. See Tim's [link ](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/30227454/failed-activate-for-python3-with-anaconda-on-windows-8-1)above for why.

`PS C:\> New-Item -Path $profile -ItemType File -Force`
This creates a script at:
`PS C:\> echo $profile`
...something like `C:\Users\yourUser\Documents\WindowsPowerShell\Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1`
Edit that script.
`PS C:\> explorer $profile`
Add this code, save, and reopen powershell (or `. $profile` ) :
```
function Invoke-CmdScript {
  param(
    [String] $scriptName
  )
  $cmdLine = """$scriptName"" $args & set"
  & $Env:SystemRoot\system32\cmd.exe /c $cmdLine |
  Select-String '^([^=]*)=(.*)$' | ForEach-Object {
    $varName = $_.Matches[0].Groups[1].Value
    $varValue = $_.Matches[0].Groups[2].Value
    Set-Item Env:$varName $varValue
  }
}
$condaRoot = "$Env:USERPROFILE\Anaconda3"
function Conda-Activate([string]$condaEnv) {Invoke-CmdScript $condaRoot\Scripts\activate.bat $condaEnv}
function Conda-Deactivate {Invoke-CmdScript $condaRoot\Scripts\deactivate.bat}
```
Usage: 
```
C:\> Conda-Activate TFTheano
C:\> Conda-Activate root
C:\> conda info --envs
```

Disclaimers: Deactivate, as written, didn't do the job for me, thus I just use Conda-Activate to move around. Also, I don't suspect there are security cautions with adding Invoke-Cmd to profile, so please chime in if 2 cents.

So @moonshoes87 you can fix the PATH issue by somewhat throwing caution to the wind and adding all subdirectories of programs to PATH as described [here](http://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/17715/how-can-i-set-all-subdirectories-of-a-directory-into-path). This is considered somewhat hazardous as it allows malicious ware to add a exe or subdirectory to path without needing sudo and makes it easier to accidentally add malicious ware to PATH. Though this is mostly true for setting a directory to path outside a protected folder in the first place which already happens in most traditional developer installs so it seems like a mote point. 

Alternatively we could set up symbolic links that take advantage of the protected directories in PATH and redirect you out of /usr/bin/local or whatever to where you're going. They exist on all platforms described here for [Linux and Mac](https://kb.iu.edu/d/abbe) and [Windows](https://www.howtogeek.com/howto/16226/complete-guide-to-symbolic-links-symlinks-on-windows-or-linux/). This would be more work for us but should make it easier for users trying to set up a dev install as we could automate it to a script.

What is your metadata bound to? Presumably you ran `Base.metadata.bind = engine` somewhere with a raw engine object otherwise `create_all()` would not work with no arguments. An engine will not be managed by the ZTE.

Similarly, if you run `create_all` with a connection pulled from the session there's no reason to believe it would be managed by the ZTE either so `request.tm.commit()` doesn't do anything. This is because the connection itself inherits `autocommit=False` from the session and thus you must commit the connection directly (which never happens since you don't hold a reference to it). See http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/orm/session_api.html#sqlalchemy.orm.session.Session.connection for information about that.

`request.tm.commit()` will invoke a commit if you are executing stuff on the **session that is joined to the transaction via the ZTE**. Anything else will not be managed by the ZTE / `request.tm`. From the docs linked above you may be able to start the transaction on the session, then grab the underlying connection. Since the transaction was started on session then the ZTE may manage it. Alternatively you could use the engine object directly via `create_all(DBSession().get_bind())` which should commit at the end.

For example, assume `MatA` has shape (B, 1, C) and `MatB` also has shape (B, 1, C). Our goal is to get the `ret` of size (B, 1, 1) by dot-multiply the corresponding row-vectors in two matrices.

There are two ways to achieve this:

1) Elementwise multiplication and use `mx.nd.sum`
```python
ret = mx.nd.sum(MatA * MatB, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
```
2) Use `mx.nd.batch_dot`
```python
ret = mx.nd.batch_dot(MatA, MatB, transpose_b=True)
```

Binary Serialization (using `BinaryFormatter`) is problematic and wasn't included in .NET Core originally. However, has come back with 2.0 as part of matching the .NET Standard 2.0 api set.

However all help pages about [Binary Serilization](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/standard/serialization/binary-serialization) carry this warning

> Binary serialization can be dangerous. Never deserialize data from an untrusted source and never round-trip serialized data to systems not under your control.

And suggest always using a different type of serialization (e.g. JSON, protobuf, xml etc)

[Secure Coding Guidelines](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/standard/security/secure-coding-guidelines) has the following recommendation

> ### Securing Resource Access
> When designing and writing your code, you need to protect and limit the access that code has to resources, especially when using or invoking code of unknown origin. So, keep in mind the following techniques to ensure your code is secure:
>
> * Do not use Code Access Security (CAS).
> * Do not use partial trusted code.
> * Do not use .NET Remoting.
> * Do not use Distributed Component Object Model (DCOM).
> * Do not use binary formatters.
>
> Code Access Security and Security-Transparent Code will not be supported as a security boundary with partially trusted code. We advise against loading and executing code of unknown origins without putting alternative security measures in place. The alternative security measures are:
>
> * Virtualization
> * AppContainers
> * Operating system (OS) users and permissions
> * Hyper-V containers

WCF has a long section on [Security Considerations for Data](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/framework/wcf/feature-details/security-considerations-for-data) that's probably worth reading as its recommendations are applicable to more areas than just WCF.

>>     crypto is a built-in Node.js module, why did we even attempt to serialize it?
> That is a good question we need to look into. It's def in our list of whitelisted modules. So something 'interesting' is going on and we def need to debug through to find out why this happened.

Actually, from looking at our code, we don't actually special case this at all in terms of capturing.  @lukehoban Do you remember if this was intentional.  From looking at our tests, it seems like we intended this, esp due to the confusion around what it means to use an externally imported module, inside a cloud-callback function.

I think a major thing we often hit was: what happens with a mutable/stateful module that is captured?  If you reify, then there is confusion because people don't see the state they mutated (which, is the pulumi-config case).  If you don't reify, then you cause serialization, which is only going to really work if the lib is designed for that.

So we settled on capture (and require/import on the inside if needed).

--

Note: its possible we could have a hybrid world.  One where we whitelist and *extremely* safe subset of modules to use.  i.e. things that are effectively stateless and which effectively feel "very safe" to reify.  But i'm not sure we need to do that, and i'd like to discuss things more before making any changes here that may not be necessary.

I wrote a bit about this in #397; copy and pasted (with a few edits) below.

I think there are valid cases for using a trampoline other than virtual methods; the most common one I've run into several times is wanting to expose protected methods that I can't access, and occasionally to provide a method or two that wrap the C++ method interface in some more pythonic way.

As long as I don't use members or multiple inheritance, I can already do that using:

```C++
.def("method", &Trampoline::method);
```
but there are also times when it would be nice to a local trampoline variable for whatever reason.

Another case that seems quite useful is to be able to use constructors and destructors to do instance cleanup related to the pybind11 interface layer that really have little to do with the wrapped C++ class. In #327, for instance (where the reporter wanted a `__del__`), a less intrusive solution than using the destructor of the base class would have been to declare a trampoline class and use its destructor.

Now all of that said, perhaps I'm better off wrapping the base class myself doing something like:
```C++
class MyC : public C {
    // ctors/dtor/members/etc.
};
```
and then, if I need virtual overrides, *also* declare a
```C++
class PyC : public MyC { ... };
```
but that seems rather redundant to me: this is basically aimed at dealing with that redundancy.

Perhaps, however, the performance penalty is steep enough that the "double trampoline" approach is the way to go, though, despite its inelegance.

(The above also has an inheritance problem, but it's basically the same problem trampoline classes have now, and is probably solvable using the same templated approach the docs discuss for (current) trampoline classes).

OK, I have no objections if `PyObject_IsTrue` is deemed too broad. If the convertible types are being restricted, then I'd vote for @wjakob's solution from https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/pull/925#issuecomment-311338635: it's a simplification and also more strict (no sequence or mapping types allowed). As a bonus, it doesn't require any workarounds for PyPy.

@jofusa recently wrote-up and shared [this handy benchmark](http://activisiongamescience.github.io/2016/06/15/Kafka-Client-Benchmarking/) comparing pykafka, kafka-python, and confluent-kafka-python. The full thing is worth a read, but these timings speak for themselves. I've cleaned up the tables (rounded the numbers) and sorted each by msgs/s.

Consumer timings:

| consumer                             | time              | MBs/s               | msgs/s                 |
| -------------------------------------|-------------------|---------------------|-------------------------|
| confluent-kafka-python        |	3.83	              | 24.93               | 261,408                |
| pykafka (rdkafka)                 |	6.09               | 15.67               | 164,311               |
| kafka-python                        | 26.56             | 3.59                | 37,668                 |
| pykafka (python)                  | 29.43             | 3.24                | 33,977                  |

Producer timings:

| producer                             | time              | MBs/s               | msgs/s                |
| -------------------------------------|-------------------|--------------------|------------------------|
| confluent-kafka-python        |	5.45	              | 17.50               | 183,456              |
| pykafka (rdkafka)                 |	15.72             | 6.06                 | 63,595                |
| pykafka (python)                  | 57.31             | 1.66                | 17,446                 |
| kafka-python                        | 67.86             | 1.40                | 14,737                 |

It seems like his timings have confirmed what our benchmarks also proved: that librdkafka provides huge speedups across-the-board. So based on the data, that would confirm @ottomata's thought that whatever the Python community unifies around, it should be something librdkafka-based, as these benchmarks are pretty hard to ignore.

A) Looking at the results from [Travis](https://travis-ci.org/certbot/certbot/builds/255963268), it looks like "Locking File Windows Library" is printed on Linux. See https://travis-ci.org/certbot/certbot/jobs/255963273#L1047. I think the reason for this is the `print` statement in the body of the `LockFileWindows` class executes when the class is defined which always happens. Perhaps you should put it in `__init__` so it is only run when an instance of the class is initialized?

B) I don't have a strong preference. I suspect we'll end up depending on the library unconditionally as this makes things significantly easier for packaging. With this in mind, one of the biggest things we need is for the library to be widely available if we're going to depend on it unconditionally. For this, I'd look at `fasteners`, `portalocker`, or `zc.lockfile`. `fasteners` seems to be the most established and widely used but I'd be open to others if there are significant benefits.

C) I haven't taken a look at this yet, but I'm happy to when this PR is ready for review.

D) Doing this locally will probably be a bit difficult on Windows. See our [Developer Guide](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/contributing.html#getting-started) if you haven't already for how this works on non-Windows systems. Differences will be:

1. You don't need to run `certbot-auto --os-packages-only` (assuming you have Python installed).
2. `tools/venv.sh` the Bash script won't work for you. The first reason for this is the Apache plugin will attempted to be installed which won't work on Windows. Otherwise, you should be able to execute equivalent commands in your command prompt though if you have [virtualenv](https://virtualenv.pypa.io/en/stable/installation/) installed or you replace `virtualenv` with `python -m venv` where `python` is Python 3.4+.
3. Running our full test suite (with `tox -e environment`) probably won't work on Windows since Certbot doesn't work on Windows yet. Instead, I'd encourage you to run the tests for the individual files you're modifying as described in our Developer Guide.
4. Getting integration tests to work will be difficult as networking on Windows is very different. I suggest you skip doing this for now.

You are welcome to add new scripts or instructions for how to do this on Windows as long as it it doesn't negatively impact the workflow of people on other systems.

I looked into this recently and the problem here is quite subtle:

1. Setting via `A1(virtue='BAD')` invokes a `hold_trait_notifications` context.
2. Cross validation never occurs inside this context `=>` validation of default values cannot occur here.
	+ In other words dynamic defaults created in the context **never** get validated. So even if A1 had a dynamic default of "BAD" via `@default` (which is normally validated) the problem noted above would still persist.
3. The fact that invalid default values are allowed to generate inside `hold_trait_notifications` (dynamic or otherwise) means that they will be used to determine whether change notifications should occur in `TraitType.set`.
	+ This implies that the failure of `A1(virtue=invalid)` to raise is actually an edge case where `invalid` happens to equal `A1.virtue.default_value` and a change notification is never sent to `HasTraits.notify_change`. That failure to notify means that `hold_trait_notifications` never retraces it in the cache of changes.
	+ In short, if we chose `A1(virtue="EVIL")` an error would be raised.

If you take all this together, and we say that validating default isn't something that we will guarantee, then the bug here is actually that `hold_trait_notifications` operates under the assumption that all prior trait values have been validated.

cc: @SylvainCorlay

> That means client code will have to update each instance of GraphAPI(). A way to change the default DEFAULT_VERSION means client code will only have to change in one place.

This makes sense, but I don't think your current pull request accomplishes this.

If this is the goal, it would make more sense to have a variable (called `DEFAULT_API_VERSION` or something similar) that was defined globally (like `VALID_API_VERSIONS`), not a new variable defined in `GraphAPI`.

> I agree that this patch does do two, only tangentially-related things. But changing the default from 2.0 to something else, while intrusive to clients, is simply a reaction to the face that FB will stop supporting 2.0 very soon.

You change the default from 2.0 (the oldest supported version) to 2.7 (the newest version). This isn't in line with how this SDK handles versions (the oldest supported version is always the default, which is why the default is 2.0 and not 2.6 right now).

I am aware of the removal of version 2.0, but these changes break backwards compatibility without a sufficient increase in new functionality, especially since:
  - changing the `default_version` currently used from 2.0 to 2.1 is a one line fix, and
  - if you send an API call with an unsupported version (e.g. 1.0), Facebook will automatically update it the oldest supported version for your application, so having an old "default version" does not break anything.

I think the idea of having the default version defined globally is interesting and worth pursuing (in order to make it easier for people to override the default version), and I would like to see a pull request that implemented that.

As there have been a few issues as PRs around this topic (#166, #184, #155, #190), we should probably make a decision on what to do

my 2¢ on these two cases:

**Adaptive Pooling**
Users might want to using adaptive pooling when passing images of a different size to the pretrained classifier, however, if we enable this by default the models will work with variable image sizes without the user being explicit and can exhibit degradation silently (as the model was trained with a different image size). For this reason, I think it should be explicit in the users code that they want this behavior, either by a constructor arg or doing some model surgery.

I think the model surgery is quite straight forward, and can be well documented in a tutorial/example. IMO the cost of cluttering the constructor + the docs to support this case is not worth it as it reads almost/if not more clearly by the user being explicit by doing:
```Python
resnet = torchvision.models.resnet18()
resnet.avgpool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d(1)
```

The one problem with this is that in some models the final pooling is done using the functional interface (e.g. [densenet](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/master/torchvision/models/densenet.py#L159)), but this can be easily remedied by creating members on the module for the feature pooling (and is backwards compatible)

**Fully Convolutional Networks**
In this case the network surgery required is a little more complex, but again something we can clearly document in an example for reference. Additionally, in most cases people who want a FCN will be modifying the network structure for their new task and will likely have to do some network surgery anyway.

What are your thoughts @fmassa @soumith @colesbury?

So @Tarrasch is correct, the string check within`parse` won't warn the user. However, we cannot just simply move that string check into `serialize`.

The problem with including the warn within `serialize` is that other Parameters inherit (and don't override) that method. e.g. `FloatParameter`, `IntParameter`, and `BoolParmeter`.

If we add the string check within `Parameter.serialize`, we will need to override the `serialize` method for int, float, and bool.

I like where this is going, but I think we're actually losing some flexibility by enforcing `isalpha()` and `isdigit()`, etc. Right now, you can subclass `_timelex()` and override the relevant character sets, but in your version, that's no longer possible.

That said, I think that this *is* the right *default* approach. Today I'll make a PR against your branch with a suggested improvement.

I agree with this. The ISO 8601 spec does not specify the precision of fractional components (this is left to "mutual agreement"). My design principle is that we will be liberal about anything not explicitly forbidden by the spec.

I don't think there's any way to do better than truncation while still returning standard datetimes.

Let's start with expanding the tests to cover up to 12 digits (should be very easy) and the corresponding fixes that makes the tests pass. We'll consider the UI of how to specify the precision later.

> This happens because dateutil tries to make some context-dependent guesses about what time zone you mean when the time zone is ambiguous.

That makes sense, in general, but for a timestamp like `"2017-02-23T06:54:00Z"`, the time zone isn't ambiguous, [it's UTC][1]. The resulting `datetime` object really shouldn't be dependent on the system timezone.

Incidentally, it's not clear to me that #356 is a duplicate of this issue; in the snippet pasted there, it looks like the parser is returning a datetime in the wrong timezone altogether. AFAIK, GMT is never equivalent to UTC+01. British Summer Time is, of course, but that's a separate time zone. Some countries, such as Iceland, use GMT year-round and don't observe DST.

[1]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601#UTC

>Well, if you forget to do either it'll complain at your in the type check

Correct! Except if one side is `Any` then it would not complain. Notice that this is a bit dangerous and one reason why we want to move away from implicit `Any` and enable a stricter mypy check.

Regarding casting: Casting is usually less type safe. In general, casting means to overrule type checking so I would *assume* the following to work and cause trouble at runtime.

`cast('foo', Blocknumber)`

In contrast the following should (rightfully) not work. 

`Blocknumber('foo')`

I would like to also point out that sshuttle appears to deal very poorly with load balancing multiple connections.

That is, if one client (or browser tab, or "update" process) is using a lot of bandwidth, there is often *zero* bandwidth available to other processes.  Other processes are not merely slow, they are completely starved for bandwidth - even if they are older than the process that is hogging all the bandwidth.

So for instance, if you are running sshuttle as a network gateway (like I do) you might have 2-3 client systems running Internet traffic through the gateway and as long as nobody needs a lot of bandwidth, they seem to play together nicely.

But then, let's say one of those clients (an apple laptop) decides to do a software update, then you'll see a new connection to aaplimg.com (for instance) using ALL available bandwidth and no other Internet access is possible.  Every other new connection will simply time out until the bandwidth hog is finished.

```python
# Ensure all data has been converted to a supported type
if data:
    for k, v in data.items():
        if isinstance(v, CheckboxValues):
            v = list(v)
        data[k] = v

Took a quick look to track down where the exception originates in the C code.  It is being raised by the following shape check.
https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/492c4343cc19ea1c6a5d77e35ef8851d2828b11e/pywt/_extensions/c/wt.template.c#L56-L57
When an exception was raised I found input_shape[0] was 16 as expected, but output_shape[0] was nonsensical values such as 659753992! 

I think the culprit may be the following lines:
https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/492c4343cc19ea1c6a5d77e35ef8851d2828b11e/pywt/_extensions/_swt.pyx#L138-L141
when cA is reassigned later in the function, this original cA may get garbage collected leading to bad pointers in output_shape.  I have a fix that seems to be working locally and will make a PR.

So that's because you're trying to pass a GroupName attribute to the Parameter type - which isn't a part of that type:

http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/parameters-section-structure.html

troposphere follows the CloudFormation API as closely as it can.  A Parameter isn't a SecurityGroup.

I'm actually not sure which object you are using that does have a GroupName - in cloudformation here's the definition of a security group:

http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-ec2-security-group.html

Honestly, at this point you've heavily modified that template - this bit of code:

```
    SecurityGroup = template.add_parameter(Parameter(
        "SecurityGroup",
        Type="String",
        Description="Security group for api instances.",
    ))
```

Does not setup a security group. It sets up a Parameter, that you can then later reference (Ref) in your template for other objects.  So say you want to allow people to be able to provide a VpcID to a security Group (this is the best example, I'm not entirely sure if you can name resources via Parameters/Refs, so naming a security group via a Ref is a bad idea):

```
template.add_parameter(Parameter(
  "VpcID",
  Type="String",
  Description="The VPC ID"))

template.add_resource(SecurityGroup(
  "MySecurityGroup",
  GroupDescription="My SEcurity Group!",
  VpcId=Ref("VpcID"))) # <--- note this refers to the parameter VpcID above

```

Does that make sense?

I ran in to this exact issue. I believe this issue only exists when one tries to run localstack the `docker-compose up` way. 

When we're passing in /var/run/docker to localstack we're effectively using our hosts docker inside the container, meaning that the volume mounts in any containers created inside localstack will be mounted from the host and not the container itself. 
A workaround is to create the directory /tmp/localstack on your host maching and mount it in the docker-compose.yml for localstack. You will then get the files inside the lambda container (and on your host...)

By then you're probably able to execute the lambda function, but you might have other issues depending on what you're trying to do. In my case I'd like to access the localstack S3 API and to do that I first have to figure out the internal IP address of the localstack container which can be accessed from within the lambda container. I can then set the s3 endpoint inside of the lambda function by doing: 

`const s3 = new aws.S3({
  apiVersion: '2007-03-01',
  endpoint: '172.21.0.2:4572'
});`

Problem is though that even though I'm able to access 172.21.0.2:4572 I do not have a host setup for  the full endpoint which is BUCKET.172.21.0.2:4572.

So here I'm getting this error:
`Error: getaddrinfo ENOTFOUND testbucket.172.21.0.2`

I tried to access /etc/hosts to add a row from within node.js but the user we get in the lambdaci/lambda:node6.10 image doesn't have the rights to edit that file. I also tried to setup 172.21.0.2:4572 as a [proxy](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/sdk-for-javascript/v2/developer-guide/node-configuring-proxies.html) but so far this is timing out every time.

While this might be entirely due to localstack running in docker, and lambda as a docker in docker container, installing the entire stack locally is not an option for me. Any suggestions are greatly appreciated.

@GiedriusM, thanks for raising this issue.  Thread currently uses a randomly generated MAC address to minimize the chance of MAC address duplication and enhance privacy.  Currently, Thread only has text around setting the u/l bit appropriately and does not talk about the i/g bit.  I'll raise an issue on the Thread spec to see what others think about the i/g bit.  Note that there has been some debate about whether setting the u/l and i/g bits are in fact useful (see [RFC 7136](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7136) for a similar discussion about IPv6 IIDs).

We could implement a variant of this as a workaround for Python 2.6:

```python
try:
    from subprocess import STDOUT, check_output, CalledProcessError
except ImportError:  # pragma: no cover
    # python 2.6 doesn't include check_output
    # monkey patch it in!
    import subprocess
    STDOUT = subprocess.STDOUT

    def check_output(*popenargs, **kwargs):
        if 'stdout' in kwargs:  # pragma: no cover
            raise ValueError('stdout argument not allowed, '
                             'it will be overridden.')
        process = subprocess.Popen(stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
                                   *popenargs, **kwargs)
        output, _ = process.communicate()
        retcode = process.poll()
        if retcode:
            cmd = kwargs.get("args")
            if cmd is None:
                cmd = popenargs[0]
            raise subprocess.CalledProcessError(retcode, cmd,
                                                output=output)
        return output
    subprocess.check_output = check_output

    # overwrite CalledProcessError due to `output`
    # keyword not being available (in 2.6)
    class CalledProcessError(Exception):

        def __init__(self, returncode, cmd, output=None):
            self.returncode = returncode
            self.cmd = cmd
            self.output = output

        def __str__(self):
            return "Command '%s' returned non-zero exit status %d" % (
                self.cmd, self.returncode)
    subprocess.CalledProcessError = CalledProcessError
```

Adjusting the playback speed of video is a bit stupid, I'd hope if that ever became something in streamlink it remained an option and off by default.  

Part of the issue with this is streamlink doesn't do connection pipeline like a browser would, there is a cost to establish a connection and tear it down.   I'm surprised they aren't running into serious buffering problems, or maybe they are.  

Buffers are there for a reason, otherwise they should be using chunked encoding or allow rtmp to clients.  _injest_ is however a completely different thing especially with IRL.  Bit OT for here though.

Fun fact, not all players can handel discontinuity in stream.  Timestamps for example confuse mpv/ffmpeg.  How bad depends on where it happened but three common ones are packets out of order, packets delayed and packets with the same timestamps.  All of which can happen due to multipathing on the Internet.   They aren't lost.

```
V: 00:00:04 / 00:01:16 (3%) A-V:  2.461 ct:  0.246

1. Right, that's the drawback. At least, there will be no more mess with attributes naming (e.g. `base_arch` in nailgun and `basearch` in API) and any python-reserved words now can be used as attribute (e.g. `type`).
2. Basically, that was one of main ideas - to make nailgun more independent of API changes. No more changes to naigun are required if the set of attributes was changed. And it's easier to fix the same calls in robottelo than write the fix for different set of attributes for different product versions in nailgun. Just one quote about maintenance of approach with explicit parameters:

> Imagine that the structure of arguments accepted by the server changes. In this case, a version comparison must be done in the method and each possible layout must be listed.

> Imagine that the set of arguments accepted by the server changes. In this case, some painful hack must be performed, such as defining several nearly identical methods and binding one of them to an object at instantiation-time based on the server software version.

You can read more about why this approach was selected [here](https://github.com/SatelliteQE/nailgun/issues/41#issuecomment-125261222), Jeremy perfectly described all the pros and cons of each approach.
