#define base URL and the range of PDF IDs to look over
urlBase = "http://target-website.com"
urlID = range(26155, 50000)

for link in urlID:
    urlPDF = str(urlBase)+str(link)
    driver.get(urlPDF)
    
    try:
        pdfLink = driver.find_element_by_css_selector("#download")
        pdfLink.click()

        fileName = str(link)+'.pdf'
        
        if os.path.isfile('IMAGE.pdf') is True:
            os.rename("IMAGE.pdf", str(fileName))
        
        if os.path.isfile('document.pdf') is True:
            os.rename('document.pdf', str(fileName))
        
    except NoSuchElementException:
        continue
    
    fileNew = drive.CreateFile({'title': str(fileName), 'parents': [{'id': folderID}]})
    fileNew.SetContentFile(str(fileName))
    fileNew.Upload()

    os.remove(str(fileName))

I think I see the confusion. ``iterations`` is the number of different instantiations of the model for each parameter combination -- it doesn't affect the number of steps each instantiation is run for. 

With your example, if ``param_vals = {"option": ["dec", "inc"], "amount": [1,2]}`` and ``iterations=1``, there will be four model instantiations, one for each combination: ("dec", 1), ("dec", 2), ("inc," 1) and ("inc", 2). Each model will then run for ``max_steps`` steps. When you set ``iterations=5``, there will be 20 model instantiations, five for each of the four combinations above -- and each one of those instantiations will still run for ``max_steps`` steps.

Yes. ICMP-type and ICMP-code are encoded exactly like port numbers. -> NumericString (always!)
Type 9 - TCP flags and Type 12 - Fragment: are encoded as BinaryString (defined on page 8 of rfc5575) - no comparison available. (always)

No third type required.

Since I am reading all your messages as they come in (chat-like feeling) :-) ...

We abondend flowspec-clarification-00 already and merged everything into https://www.ietf.org/id/draft-ietf-idr-rfc5575bis-01.txt

There are only 2 types of operators. The one you call "NumericString" (rfc5575bis sec 4.2.3.) and the other that you call "BinaryString" (rfc5575bis sec 4.2.9. only used for type-9 and type-12) 

The new rfc5575bis also x-references from each flow-type to the section where the operator is defined. 

There are still ways to produce a BGP-notification with a perfectly well flow-spec update. We worked on an interop paper:
http://p4.tix.at/~cl/fs/loibl-bacher-bgp-flowspec-interop-012017.pdf

The screenshot is not from the Debian package (use of `/usr/local`). But, yes, in the Debian package, there is no more a default `/etc/exabgp/exabgp.conf` (which was empty with a comment pointing to `/usr/share/doc/exabgp/examples`) because we use that to know if we should start the daemon. This is considered as the right thing to do for daemons where no default sensible configuration file can be provided.

```
from confluent_kafka import KafkaError
from confluent_kafka.avro import AvroConsumer
from confluent_kafka.avro.serializer import SerializerError

c = AvroConsumer({'bootstrap.servers': 'idarlington.local,', 'group.id': 'my_group', 'schema.registry.url': 'http://idarlington.local:8081'})
c.subscribe(['gritServer'])
running = True
while running:
    try:
        msg = c.poll(10)
        if msg:
            if not msg.error():
                print(msg.value())
            elif msg.error().code() != KafkaError._PARTITION_EOF:
                print(msg.error())
                running = False
    except SerializerError as e:
        print("Message deserialization failed for %s: %s" % (msg, e))
        running = False
        
c.close()
```

Convolution using FFT is not necessarily faster than ordinary convolution. It depends on the relative sizes of the kernels. Both inputs must be large for FFT to be faster. If one input is ~ 1x1 compared to the other, than ordinary convolution has a time complexity of ~O(n), whereas FFTs time complexity is O(n log n). For optimal speed both methods should be considered.

They really should have just put the extra python versions in a package repo and then they'd be trivial to mix and match.  Having to pick a language-specific VM base image only really works for single-language projects.

This will only get worse as more backends are added to Snowball - there's Javascript and C# on the cards.  Not sure I see a good way to handle this, short of having branches with a different `.travis.yml`.

Having two different build systems is a maintenance headache, so we really need to pick one. Richard says he lacks the time, and I don't really understand cmake, plus we have PR #24 which updates the makefiles but not the cmake build system.

So while your contributions are appreciated, I'm proposing to back them out.  Richard suggested to open a PR for discussion of this, so I've opened #25.

Did you actually hit this issue in real use?

While Snowball can be thought of as a general purpose string manipulation language, I'm not aware of any uses that aren't the stemmers, where the only external is always called `stem` anyway.  You can call a routine `null` as routines all get an `r_` prefix (which we can easily do for routines as they're only internally visible, so we also generate all the references to them).

The sanest "fix" I can currently see is just document this as a potential issue when choosing `external` names if you're using Snowball outside of the stemmers.

@rgbkrk: There are serialization failures on my test objects under stress in `multiprocess` (note the "`dill`-aware" version, not the std-lib version) either way… a few objects pass with `recurse=True` that fail with `recurse=False` and vice versa.  There are also test failures in `dill` that don't fail in `cloudpickle` (mostly the 3rd-party objects specifically added to `cloudpickle`), and vice versa.

I don't see a generic "best" choice at the moment.  `dill` with `recurse=False` has the least amount of failures on object coverage, but that doesn't necessarily mean the best coverage on usage patterns. 

I would think that sort of monkeypatch this might go badly, but it might also work:
```
import dill as pickle
# pickle[`recurse`] = True
import cloudpickle as pickle
```
Essentially, setting `dill` as a base to get all of the `dill` objects registered,
then replacing them with any of the objects `cloudpickle`  also handles.

The best option is probably a little sprint to get `recurse=True` working for the cases from `recurse=False` that it breaks -- and also pull the existing `cloudpickle` special cases into `dill`.  Then we have unification, and in a single package that's actively maintained.  I don't think it would take too much effort to do.

I've been caught by the same issues.

In absence of regular import mechanism at discover() level, in my case it's even harder to put in place workarounds than with postgres, because I have to import from paths that contain dashes. For example consider the following charm-built layout:

    ./hooks/
    ./hooks/relations/
    ./hooks/relations/jenkins-slave/
    ./hooks/relations/jenkins-slave/requires.py
    ./hooks/relations/jenkins-slave/lib/master.py
   ./hooks/relations/jenkins-slave/unit_tests/test_master.py

In such situation it turns out that:

1. From withing requires.py it's not possible to import utilities living in lib/master.py
2. When discover() scans the tree, it will try to import test_master.py, which fails to import not only because of 1., but also because it contains test-only dependencies that are not declared in wheehouse.txt (since they are not needed at runtime).

The result being if you have two or more relations to the same service and are using UNIT or SERVICE scope, RelationBase.conversation() will return an incorrect conversation over half the time (correct scope, correct relation name, incorrect relation id/namespace)

@SeanHayes Thanks for reply and library.

I profiled our queries and found the traceback using django-silk.

I found that repeated query happening at two locations in code.
- When the_m2ms is evaluated at `for m2m in the_m2ms:` in dehydrate of m2m.
- When the given m2m has a ToOne resource related to it. It calls `dehydrate` of ToOneField which in turn calls a method `getattr(foreign_obj, attr, None)` these are two places where repeated queries happening.

And the data which those are accessing are already in prefetched/select_related.

Let me explain a bit more, we have our Resource class like this : - 
```py
class tableResource(ModelResource):
    items = ToManyField(ItemResource, attribute='items', full=True, null=True)
    class Meta:
      queryset = table.objects.using('default').select_related('table2') \
        .prefetch_related(
            Prefetch('table3',
                     queryset=table3.objects.select_related('table4', 'cols')
                     .prefetch_related('options', 'options__options')
                     .only(*vc)
                     ),
            Prefetch('items',
                     queryset=Item.objects.select_related('item_data')
                     .only(*vcx)
                     ),
            'tableX',
            'tableX__options'
       )\
        .only(*fdv)

      fields = (... our fields ...)
      always_return_data = True
```

Now as a part of `full_dehydrate` tastypie traverse each field in `self.fields`, now in process it comes to `items` filed ,tastypie saw this a M2m resource and calls dehydrate of m2m.
Now inside that dehydrate we have for loop in tastypie which is like 
`for m2m in the_m2ms:`, now at this moment since the_m2ms was a queryset it evaluates itself and fire a query doing a join on `items` and `table_items` which makes no sense[**This is an extra query**].

Now in item it iterate over all item and goes to `dehydrate_related` for each which builds a bundle and called `full_dehydrate` on `related_resource` which in our case is `itemResource`. Inside `full_dehydrate` it again iterates over `self.fields`[**self here is itemResource**]. and found `itemdata` as having toOne relation with `item` so it calls `ToOne.dehydrate` and inside that we have `getattr(foreign_obj, attr, None)` which fires an query on `item_data` model[** which is also extra **].

Note both item and item_data are part of queryset's `prefetch/select_related`

I think one solution to the problem is you can get the graph of the Graph model, modify the graph and create a new Graph model on it. We can add a getNodeByName method to Graph, then for your use case, we can

1. remove the last several layers, so now we get output from e.g. conv10
```scala
val output = graph.getNodeByName("conv10")
val input = graph.getNodeByName("input")
val newModel = Graph(input, output)
```

2. replace last layer 'fc10' with different fc layer, layer 'relu' point to 'fc10'
```scala
val node = graph.getNodeByName("relu")
val newOutput = Linear(...).inputs(node)
val input = graph.getNodeByName("input")
val newModel = Graph(intput newOutput)
```

In ipywidgets 7, the widgets are one of the outputs of the cell, just like any other outputs. That means that clearing the cell output will clear the widgets.

If you'd like to clear only a portion of output, we suggest you capture that output using an Output widget:

```
from ipywidgets import IntSlider, Output
from IPython.display import display
display(IntSlider())
out = Output()
display(out)
with out:
    print('hi')
```

Now `out.clear_output()` or 
```
from IPython.display import clear_output
with out:
    clear_output()
```
will clear just the things in that output widget.

- For frontend to backend messages the throttle applies. Here, a possible solution is to add a method `send_custom_queued` (or something like it) that adds the custom message to the queue. The recommended method to install our python bindings for fastText is the following:
```
$ git clone https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText.git
```

```python
import fastText
```

It's better to not use MPRester in matminer unit tests in the first place.  Instead serialize the structure and band structure (e.g. to JSON file in the tests dir) and load from there. Otherwise, if MP goes down then the matminer unit tests will start failing, which is not what we want (since matminer is actually still fine).

As an aside, I actually don't know the best way to set it, would need to check the CircleCI docs for instructions on either setting an environment var or executing a command (e.g., "cat PMG_MAPI_KEY=xyz > ~/.pmgrc.yaml") before running the test

If you want to stick with raising exceptions in all cases, handling the following semantics seems to cover validation.  (Although it's really ValidationError**s** now.)  This would allow adding one or more errors

~~~
# during field validation, adds one message to the errors for the field
# during schema validation, adds one message to some top-level error key
raise ValidationError('message1')

# during field validation, adds multiple messages to the errors for the field
# during schema validation, adds multiple messages to some top-level error key
raise ValidationError('message1', 'message2')

# during field and schema validation, adds message or messages to the given key
raise ValidationError(field='message1', field2=['message2', 'message3'])

# *args and **kwargs can be used together, will add messages to the field and to other fields
raise ValidationError('message1', field2='message2')
~~~

---

I am *not* saying that processing returns some "options tuple" or something different than what it already does.  Dump and load *already* return a 2-tuple `data, errors`.

---

Why shouldn't deserialization have access to permissions or whatever other supplementary data is needed?  In Flask, that sort of information is just a thread local away, you don't even need to pass in anything: `session`, `current_user`, etc.  For Django-likes that don't use thread locals, Marshmallow already allows setting context for validation.

Perhaps an alternative would be to properly expose the errors that have been captured during unmarshalling. At the moment you have to go through ```self._unmarshal.errors```. Individual validate_schema methods would either check for errors before running of the decorator could be modified to automatically do this check if configured. Of course, I could be missing something and these errors might be already getting exposed at a more desirable location.

One thing we have been considering is adding a public `.load()` method, which will allow you to write code like `annie = Champion(name='Annie').load()` or `matches = [m.load() for m in match_history]` to force an object to load. This would allow you to have very fine grained control over when data is loaded, at the expense of having to type more code. Would this solve your load balancing issue?

We will consider having `to_json()` load the data. So far, it's been very nice having `to_dict` _not_ load the data, and only showing information that is currently available. Maybe `to_json` and `to_dict` can work slightly differently, although that was never the plan. Even without having `to_json` load the data, running `annie.load().to_json()` doesn't sound like a terrible API. We will make sure `.load()` doesn't throw an error if it has already been loaded.

Side comment about using `to_json`: We never really intended `to_json` to be used to send all the data that comes from the API to your frontend. Instead, we always assumed that devs would pick out the information they wanted from an object, put that data into a dict/json, and then send only that information to their frontend. By sending the entirety of `to_json()` to the frontend, a malicious person could essentially use your website as an additional source of data, and thereby use your production API key for themselves. In addition, by constructing the dict from the data on the object, the dev can add additional information that they compute to that dict/json before sending it to the frontend.  Final comment on this, Cass is more targeted towards apps that want to manipulate data rather than just "passing through" that data from Riot to the frontend. Cass provides an interface for working with data that is meant to be extremely convenient, and that's a bit part of our goal. Although we also provide a bunch of utility stuff (like caching and databasing) that "pass through" applications will certainly benefit from.

>Also for containers of cassiopeia objects, properties for the container will be incorrect until data is populated.

So this should never be the case -- if it is, let us know and we'll almost certainly fix it. For example, calling `len` on a match history will load the entire match history before returning the length. To the user, the containers that Cass uses should act exactly as you would expect, with the exception that they only load data if/when you need it. That's why calling `len` pulls all the data.

We've been doing lazy and/or eager loading with Cass for a long time, so you'll have to trust us that we know what we're doing. The "old" Cass (and Ori) differentiated between lazy and eager loading, and we used that system for a few years. There were a few inconveniences that sprang up that we didn't particularly like, so when we started in on this version of Cass/Ori we were very careful about how we wanted to load data, and we are very happy with the results. Once we figured out the right way to handle all the different pieces, they came together very nicely, including the loading functionality.

What is very helpful is if you tell us what your use cases are and why what Cass provides is hindering those use cases (e.g. the load balancing). We've put a ridiculous amount of time and effort into thinking about functionality that we ourselves will probably never use, in order to provide a product that anyone can use for (nearly) any use case. So far, we haven't had to make too many sacrifices in order to accommodate every use case we can think of, so we're pretty confident that minor changes in functionality can enable use cases that we haven't thought of yet (while continuing to accommodate all the other use cases out there). You'll notice how many options/configuration switches there are in the documentation -- that's a sign of what we have already considered since Cass was released a few months ago.

@dhermes:

* `Connection.compact_table()` is a performance optimization (compacted tables have lower depth, hence reads are faster). Bigtable does compactions on its own schedule, so we can safely ignore this as a noop. In essence, we're saying "sure, consider it done, we've taken care of all compactions for you". No semantics difference here.

* `Table.regions()`: doesn't look like we can return a reasonable value here: returning an empty list will look like it's broken, returning a single item with the first and last key will look like we always have a single region (which is false, and we don't want any application code to assume this is the case), but we also don't want to return the actual tablet server splits, either, so this may have to remain an exception, at least for now. If any user complains about this, please feel free to send them to me.

* `Table.counter_set()`: I think we should implement it the same way that HappyBase does, but looks like there was an explicit decision made (per the comments) to avoid this, so I may be missing context here. I see the point that it appears to defeat the purpose of a "counter", which I agree with, but presumably HappyBase users had a good (?) reason for adding this method, and it wouldn't break Bigtable or HappyBase to support it as-is. Who was the original author of this? We should chat about this further.

/cc: @sduskis, @garye, @lesv, @rameshdharan — thoughts?

`run_with_transaction(callback)` sets up a transaction and passes it to `callback`, and afterwards attempts to commit the transaction.  While the `commit` back-end raises `ABORT`, and the user-supplied timeout has not expired, it retries.

In order to exercise the retry loop without adding too much delay, I set the timeout to 10 msec.  I didn't think it would be possible for the following to take longer than that:

```python
        def unit_of_work(txn):
            called_with.append((txn, args, kw))
            txn.insert(TABLE_NAME, COLUMNS, VALUES)
```

(along with the call to `txn.commit()`, which then delegates to the mocked-out `spanner_api`, raising an `Abort`).
